note: The narrated script. One idea per card, keyed to a year. Edit the text here. No em dashes or semicolons in this file. The final card (act END) closes the story and shows the Replay / Explore buttons.

chapters.text: Dozens of creeks run through forest and marsh to the lake. Every valley on this map was carved by one.
The town of York sits by the harbour. A few thousand people. The creeks are the water supply.
York becomes the City of Toronto. Nine thousand people, and growing fast.
The railway arrives. Mills and factories crowd the creek banks, and their waste goes straight into the water.
There are no real sewers yet. Privies, stables and slaughterhouses all drain downhill to the nearest creek.
Taddle Creek still pools beside the young university.
Cholera and typhoid keep returning. The creeks carry both.
The city votes to bury Garrison Creek in a brick pipe. A health hazard becomes a sewer, and the ravine becomes real estate.
Burying a creek raises the land around it. A property boom follows the sewer crews.
The last native salmon in Lake Ontario is caught off Scarborough. The fish were gone before the water was.
Toronto swallows its neighbours. Yorkville, Parkdale, the Annex. Each one brings more creeks, and the pipes follow.
In a single generation, most of this map has gone dark.
The ravines are filled with rubble and subway dirt, and parks are laid on top. The Crawford Street bridge is buried where it stands.
Some streets still curve for no reason anyone remembers. The reason is a creek.
The creeks never left. They run through pipes under these streets, and after a hard rain some still overflow into the lake.
A few stretches were never buried, or have been opened back to daylight. Those are the ones still glowing.
I still fish this lake. Now when a street dips for no reason, I know what runs beneath it. You are probably standing on a river.
